name: Porsche
philosophy: An inspired interpretation of Porsche's brand language — the Stuttgart sports-car house whose visual discipline is the opposite of every other German luxury automaker. Canvas is pure white or dark race-track-black. Type runs the proprietary Porsche Next typeface — a precise, modernist grotesk with subtle aperture variations — at every text tier. The signature is Porsche red (#d5001c) — historically reserved for the 911 GT badging, used scarcely on configurator highlights, on the wordmark in select moments, and on the iconic horse-and-shield crest. Photography is cinematic motorsport: cars at speed on Alpine roads, paddock close-ups of the 911 chassis, design-team studio portraits. The voice is engineering-first — every page reads like a Le Mans technical manual translated into web layout.

design_language.motion_signature: Cinematic motorsport. Hero videos auto-play silent: 911s carving through Pyrenees passes, slow-motion engine-bay close-ups, paddock pit-stops. Cross-fades at 1.2s — fast enough to feel kinetic but slower than Tesla's quick cuts. Hover lifts product tiles 6px with a sharp shadow (engineering precision, not soft luxury). Configurator color-changes animate the car body via 800ms color-tween.

trademark_signals: Porsche Next at every tier — the proprietary grotesk that reads as engineering-document, not fashion-magazine — refusing the serif-luxury convention
Porsche red (#d5001c) reserved for the 911 GT badging, the horse-and-shield crest, and rare configurator highlights — never used as a section background, never as a CTA fill on standard product pages
Stuttgart horse-and-shield crest — the gold-and-red coat-of-arms emblem — appears at the top of every page as a sub-mark beneath the 'PORSCHE' wordmark
Cinematic motorsport photography — 911s on Alpine roads, paddock close-ups, engine-bay portraits — refusing the polished-on-white showroom convention every other luxury brand uses
Engineering-document copy — every product page describes lateral-G performance figures, lap times, kilowatt outputs, drag coefficients — Porsche-spec, never marketing-spec
ai_slop_avoided: Don't substitute Porsche Next with a fashion-serif. The proprietary grotesk IS the engineering voice — Garamond reads as Ferrari, off-brand for Porsche
Don't paint the canvas in luxury cream. Porsche is white-or-black; cream reads as Ferrari/Rolls-Royce off-brand
Don't show cars on white turntable. Porsche photography is in-motion, on-track, in-paddock — static showroom shots read as 'generic luxury auto'
Don't drop the lap-time / spec-sheet. The engineering data IS the brand's voltage — feature-bullet marketing copy reads as off-brand
Don't decorate with Porsche red. The red is reserved for GT badging and the crest; using it on every accent moment dilutes the brand restraint
